summary: Paul Graham argues that Lisp was a radical break from Fortran-era languages because it introduced nine foundational ideas, many of which later became mainstream. He emphasizes that Lisp’s power came from treating code as data and making the whole language available at all stages, enabling macros and deep extensibility. He concludes Lisp was not a reaction to Fortran’s flaws but a byproduct of McCarthy’s effort to axiomatize computation.
key_points: Lisp introduced nine core ideas, including conditionals, first-class functions, recursion, garbage collection, expression-based programs, symbols, code-as-trees, and unified read/compile/run time.
Many of Lisp’s innovations have gradually spread into mainstream languages, though macros and code-as-data remain largely unique to Lisp.
Lisp’s design was driven by a theoretical model of computation rather than a pragmatic fix to earlier languages.
money_quotes: When McCarthy designed Lisp in the late 1950s, it was a radical departure from existing languages, the most important of which was Fortran.
Lisp programs are trees of expressions, each of which returns a value.
When a language is made entirely of expressions, you can compose expressions however you want.
Lisp wasn't designed to fix the mistakes in Fortran; it came about more as the byproduct of an attempt to axiomatize computation.
key_insight: Lisp’s lasting influence comes from a small set of deep, theoretical ideas—especially code as data and full-stage language availability—that later languages have only partially absorbed.
